# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Cherry Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from https://www.z-beam.com/materials/wood/hardwood/cherry-laser-cleaning

Typical range for wood cleaning at 1064 nm to remove contaminants without charring; starting at 50 W for delicate hardwoods like cherry to minimize thermal damage. Max safe ~200 W to avoid substrate ablation. Source: Studies on laser cleaning of wood (e.g., Journal of Cultural Heritage, 2018); IPG Photonics YLR series specs for cleaning applications.

Effective speeds for contaminant removal on wood; 500 mm/s recommended for optimal balance of cleaning efficiency and heat control on cherry wood. Slower (100-500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (1000-2000 mm/s) for light. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

Nanosecond regime preferred for wood to ablate contaminants with minimal heat-affected zone; 20 ns recommended for cherry to reduce thermal diffusion given wood's low conductivity (~0.16 W/m·K). Picosecond (ps) or femtosecond optional for ultra-precision but less common industrially. Source: Laser physics research in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2020).

Typical for pulsed cleaning of organics; 30 kHz balances speed and heat accumulation on cherry wood (reflectivity ~40% at 1064 nm, melting point N/A but charring >200°C). Higher rates increase speed but risk buildup. Source: Academic literature, e.g., Applied Surface Science (2019) on wood laser processing.

Fluence range for safe contaminant removal; cherry wood damage threshold ~8-12 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to low thermal conductivity. 1.5 J/cm² recommended for cleaning without substrate harm. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2017) for hardwoods.

Beam diameter for precise cleaning; 200 μm recommended for cherry surface preparation to cover details without overexposure. Smaller for fine work, larger for broad areas. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems.

Multi-pass for thorough removal on wood; 2 passes recommended for cherry to ensure complete contamination clearance while monitoring for discoloration. Single pass for light soils. Source: Industry case studies in restoration (e.g., ICALEO proceedings, 2021).
Ensures uniform coverage; 60% recommended for cherry to avoid gaps or excessive overlap heating, given wood's sensitivity. Source: Process optimization in Laser & Photonics Reviews (2016).
